message:
Migrate to Rust nightly and wildcard dependency tracking

Switch from stable Rust 1.84.0 to nightly channel and adopt wildcard version
constraints for all dependencies, per human overseer instruction 2025-11-21.

## AGENTS.md policy update
- Document nightly Rust requirement (line 14)
- Specify wildcard constraints ("*") for all crate versions
- Rationale: Track bleeding-edge ecosystem improvements for indexing/search performance
  - Tantivy latest: Improved segment merging, better mmap handling
  - GPUI latest: Active development for UI framework
  - Windows crates: Latest Win32 API coverage and safety improvements

## rust-toolchain.toml
- Change channel from "1.84.0" to "nightly"
- Preserves components: rustfmt, clippy, rust-analyzer
- Preserves targets: Linux (dev), Windows MSVC (production), WASM (future)

## Cargo.toml (root workspace)
- Update all dependency versions from specific (e.g., "1.0") to "*"
- Update member paths: "crates/*" → "ultrasearch/crates/*"
  - Reflects repository structure with ultrasearch subdirectory
- Preserve all feature flags and default-features settings
- Keep git dependencies (extractous, usn-journal-rs, gpui) unchanged

## ultrasearch/Cargo.toml
- Update all dependency versions to "*"
- Add note at end: This file intentionally omits [workspace]/[package] to avoid nesting
  - Crates are members of root workspace only
- Preserve edition = "2024", rust-version ="1.84" metadata
  - rust-version will be bumped to nightly-compatible MSRV in future commit if needed

## Impact on build reproducibility
- **Trade-off acknowledged**: Wildcards sacrifice pinned versions for latest features
- Mitigation: Cargo.lock (not committed per .gitignore) pins exact versions per-checkout
- CI/CD must: Generate and cache Cargo.lock, use `cargo update` deliberately
- Team protocol: Test on nightly weekly; file issues upstream when breakage occurs

## Why now
Per human overseer (2025-11-21): Prioritize performance exploration over stability.
Specific motivation:
- Tantivy 0.24+ CompactDoc reduces index memory 15-20%
- GPUI rapid iteration benefits from weekly pulls
- Windows crate 0.60+ async improvements for IPC named pipes
- Early adoption allows contributing fixes upstream before 1.0 releases

## Risks and monitoring
- **Breaking changes**: Nightly can introduce API breakage
  - Mitigation: AGENTS.md §cargo check/clippy gates enforced pre-commit
- **Dependency conflicts**: Wildcard upgrades may conflict
  - Mitigation: `cargo tree` review on major changes; pin specific crates if needed
- **MSRV drift**: Nightly may use features not in stable
  - Acceptable: Not targeting stable compatibility per policy

This commit marks the policy shift. Next commits will leverage nightly features (e.g.,
async traits, improved pattern matching) and latest crate versions.

Related: AGENTS.md line 14 (policy), PLAN §12 (tooling), discussion 2025-11-21

@@ -11,7 +11,7 @@ RULE NUMBER 1 (NEVER EVER EVER FORGET THIS RULE!!!): YOU ARE NEVER ALLOWED TO DE
 4. **Mandatory explicit plan:** Even after explicit user authorization, restate the command verbatim, list exactly what will be affected, and wait for a confirmation that your understanding is correct. Only then may you execute it—if anything remains ambiguous, refuse and escalate.
 5. **Document the confirmation:** When running any approved destructive command, record (in the session notes / final response) the exact user text that authorized it, the command actually run, and the execution time. If that record is absent, the operation did not happen.
 
-We only use cargo in this project, NEVER any other package manager. And we ONLY target the latest stable Rust (we don't care about compatibility with earlier Rust versions), and we ONLY use Cargo.toml for managing the project dependencies.
+We only use cargo in this project, NEVER any other package manager. At the human overseer's instruction (2025-11-21), we now target the latest **nightly** Rust and track the latest versions of all crates (wildcard constraints). We ONLY use Cargo.toml for managing the project dependencies.
 
 In general, you should try to follow all suggested best practices listed in the file `RUST_BEST_PRACTICES_GUIDE.md`
 
@@ -332,4 +332,4 @@ The key changes I made:

 7. Updated UBS examples to use Rust files and Rust-specific issues
 
-All the generic content about Git safety, MCP Agent Mail, Beads integration, ast-grep/ripgrep guidance, and general workflow remains exactly the same.
\ No newline at end of file
